commit.message:
fix: shorten newsletter URL params to stay under Mailgun 1000-char tracking limit

Mailgun silently skips click-tracking for href values >= 1000 characters.
Our newsletter URLs with authToken JWT were ~1010-1087 chars, causing
only 7/24 links to be tracked (those with shorter paths).

Changes:
- newsletter_email -> ne (saves 14 chars)
- authToken -> at (saves 7 chars)
- Drop newsletter_autologin param (always 1, saves 24 chars)
- Drop newsletter_source param (always 'weekly', saves 26 chars)
- Drop subscriber_key param (redundant with email, saves 40 chars)
- Revert & encoding (not the root cause)
- Total savings: ~111 chars -> max URL now ~976 chars

Client-side (App.tsx, authService.ts, build-plugins) reads both old
and new param names for backwards compatibility with cached emails.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Rework ballscrew joint model to match physical design

Replace old slider-crank (A/B pivot on thigh/shank) with the accurate
model: motor on top of upper-leg tube drives a nut up/down the shaft;
nut connects through a slot to the shank at distance R_ARM from the
knee pivot.

Key changes:
- leg_kinematics.py: replace ballscrew_length/velocity with
  nut_position(), nut_lever_arm(), nut_velocity(), nut_stroke_for_range()
  using virtual-work relationship F = tau / (R_ARM * |sin theta|)
- force_calculator.py: rename functions (nut_force_required,
  motor_torque_from_nut_force, motor_speed_rpm, motor_power);
  add CARRIAGE_DRAG_N=0 parameter; add motor RPM/power outputs
- main.py: update summary table with nut stroke, lever arm, screw params
- pybullet_sim.py: import CARRIAGE_DRAG_N

Results: peak nut force ~774 N, motor torque ~0.69 N*m (5mm lead, 90% eff)
Nut stroke: 92.7 mm over working range, 37.6 mm during gait cycle.

commit.message:
consensus: fix TOCTOU race in validate_block_rewards causing false reward-injection rejects

Step 2 was calling the live tier_for_wallet (fresh registry read) while Step 5
used the all_infos snapshot captured at the start of validation.  A masternode
that gossip-registered between those two reads would be classified as 'known'
in Step 2 (keeping unknown_non_producer_paid == 0, so Step 5 ran) but absent
from all_infos, causing the is_known_masternode fallback in Step 5 to return
false and hard-reject a fully valid block.

Fix: replace the live tier_for_wallet call with snapshot_tier_for_wallet, a
closure that searches the already-captured all_infos slice.  Steps 2 and 5 now
use the same registry snapshot.  If a wallet is absent from the snapshot,
Step 2 increments unknown_non_producer_paid, which causes Step 5's strict
bitmap address check to be skipped (the correct lenient path — pool amounts
are still fully validated in Steps 2-4).

This was causing the node to stall at height 1092, rejecting block 1093 from
all peers including whitelisted nodes, with spurious 'possible reward injection'
warnings for legitimate Silver-pool recipient TIME15E85jKLtMsWax7oQVTHy8toXHHVauxj8H.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Add CatArrayBatchedCopy_contig relative kernels (#1821)

https://github.com/intel/torch-xpu-ops/issues/1764
**Improvements**
- Fixed incorrect indexing behavior for channels-last non-contiguous cat
inputs.
- Aligned non-contiguous input metadata mapping with the same
logical-dimension semantics used by the output path.
- Preserved fast-path behavior for contiguous and channels-last
contiguous cases.
- Maintained replay-level throughput with slight overall improvement.

**Graph-Level Layout Coverage and Impact (Conv + Concat)**
- Before this fix, channels-last propagation was incomplete in the
conv+concat graph family.
- Cat-output channels-last coverage:
Overall across the 4 graph variants: 25% (1/4) -> 100% (target
deployment path).
Within channels-last-input graph variants: 33.3% (1/3) -> 100% (target
deployment path).
- Once the graph stays fully channels-last end-to-end, measured gains
versus mixed-layout states are:
Against channels_last_to_channel_first_cat:
conv mean: 266.33 us -> 148.58 us (1.79x faster, -44.2%)
cat mean: 101.55 us -> 65.44 us (1.55x faster, -35.6%)
Against channels_last_cat_then_channel_first_out:
conv mean: 298.30 us -> 148.58 us (2.01x faster, -50.2%)
cat mean: 122.86 us -> 65.44 us (1.88x faster, -46.7%)

**Baseline Performance**
Replay-based concat latency is effectively unchanged after the fix.
Aggregate geomean: 1.000x -> 1.004x.
Per-case movement is within a small range (approximately -1.4% to
+0.9%).
Conclusion: the correctness fix is performance-neutral at baseline
scale, with no observable regression.

* fix: forecast not found after job finishes due to JSONB key-order hash mismatch

refresh_data_source() was calling get_or_create_source() with attributes
loaded from PostgreSQL JSONB. JSONB serializes JSON object keys in alphabetical
order, but attributes_hash was originally computed from the Python insertion-
order dict — so the hash never matched and a brand-new DataSource was created
with a different ID. The forecasts were saved under the new ID while the job
meta still recorded the original ID, causing the search to return empty.

Fix: look up the data source by its ID first (via db.session.get), falling back
to get_or_create_source only if the ID lookup fails.

Also fix dep.decode("utf-8") in get_forecast: modern RQ returns dependency_ids
as plain strings, not bytes, so guard with isinstance(dep, bytes) before
decoding.

Co-authored-by: nhoening <1042336+nhoening@users.noreply.github.com>

* refactor: remove inline db import (already at top), simplify dep decode expression

* fix: make DataSource.hash_attributes stable under JSON key order (sort_keys=True)

PostgreSQL JSONB always returns object keys in alphabetical order, so a dict
created with Python's insertion order (e.g. {"z": 1, "a": 2}) comes back from
the DB as {"a": 2, "z": 1}. The old hash_attributes used json.dumps without
sort_keys, meaning the two orderings produced different SHA-256 digests.
get_or_create_source therefore failed to find an existing DataSource and
silently inserted a duplicate row with a different ID.

Changes:
- DataSource.hash_attributes: add sort_keys=True to json.dumps
- DataSource.__init__: reuse hash_attributes() (DRY)
- Migration a5b26c3f8e91: recompute all existing attributes_hash values
  using the new sort_keys=True function
- test_data_source: add test_get_or_create_source_stable_under_key_order
  to prove the bug and guard against regression

Agent-Logs-Url: https://github.com/FlexMeasures/flexmeasures/sessions/76c6b0aa-22f8-4bbc-8b54-d2a689546d68

Co-authored-by: Flix6x <30658763+Flix6x@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(migration): handle duplicate DataSource rows and simplify downgrade

Upgrade: before updating attributes_hash values, detect groups of DataSource
rows that share the same normalised unique key (after JSONB sorting). For each
such conflict group the newest row (highest id) is kept clean; older duplicates
get a synthetic {"flexmeasures-hash-conflict": N} attribute so their hash stays
unique -- avoiding a UniqueConstraint violation without touching timed_belief.

Downgrade: removed the hash-recomputation loop. PostgreSQL JSONB stores object
keys in alphabetical order, so json.dumps(attrs) and
json.dumps(attrs, sort_keys=True) produce the same string for any JSONB-round-
tripped attributes -- making the old loop a no-op. The downgrade is now an
intentional pass with an explanatory docstring.

Co-authored-by: Flix6x <30658763+Flix6x@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: bypass stats cache when no data found, so freshly-uploaded data is visible immediately

get_sensor_stats used a 120-second TTL LRU cache.  When a sensor had no data on
first load, the empty result was cached.  Uploading data within the 120-second
window then had no visible effect: the forecast dialog kept showing "no data"
and could not auto-populate the start datetime.

Fix: after getting an empty result from the cache, immediately do a second call
with a unique ttl_hash (time.time(), which is never reused) to force a fresh
database query.  This bypasses the stale empty cache entry without affecting
the 120-second caching for sensors that already have data.

Agent-Logs-Url: https://github.com/FlexMeasures/flexmeasures/sessions/ccc82d3a-6e28-445e-bfa9-3bff5c0483ef

Co-authored-by: nhoening <1042336+nhoening@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: re-populate normal cache slot after empty-sensor bypass to prevent repeated DB hits

After a successful bypass (empty cached result but fresh DB query finds data),
clear the stale empty LRU entry and immediately re-populate the normal TTL
slot. Subsequent calls within the 120-second window are now served from cache
instead of hitting the database each time.

The cache_clear() is acceptable here because the event is infrequent (a sensor
transitioning from empty to non-empty) and all other sensors' non-empty cache
entries will simply be re-populated on their next request.

Agent-Logs-Url: https://github.com/FlexMeasures/flexmeasures/sessions/aeb32b46-e0b2-4a83-922b-e8a299536c22

Co-authored-by: nhoening <1042336+nhoening@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: replace global lru_cache with per-key dict TTL cache for sensor stats

The previous fix used cache_clear() on a global lru_cache to evict stale
empty entries, which itself opened a DoS window: any caller could trigger
repeated global evictions.

Replace with a module-level dict (_sensor_stats_cache) that maps
(sensor_id, ...) keys to (timestamp, result) pairs:
- Non-empty results are cached per key for 120 seconds (same TTL as before)
- Empty results are stored in the dict but immediately treated as expired on
  the next call, so data uploaded to a previously-empty sensor is always
  visible without any global eviction
- Only the specific per-key entry is ever overwritten; no other sensor's
  cached data is affected

Removes: @lru_cache(), ttl_hash parameter, _get_ttl_hash() helper, and
the cache_clear() call introduced in b6576a3f / a71a975d.

Agent-Logs-Url: https://github.com/FlexMeasures/flexmeasures/sessions/a8859a89-bf08-40ee-9520-4a359ab3d0de

Co-authored-by: nhoening <1042336+nhoening@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: add max-size (1000) and time-bucket key to sensor stats cache

- Include round(time.time() / 120) in the cache key so at most one DB hit
  per sensor per 120-second window is possible; this also caps the number
  of unique keys an attacker can create to 1 per sensor per bucket, limiting
  cache-exhaustion risk.
- Cap the dict at _SENSOR_STATS_MAX_SIZE=1000 entries; when full, the oldest
  entry (FIFO insertion order) is evicted before inserting a new one.
- Empty results are still never cached (freshly-uploaded data always visible).
- Removes the explicit (populated_at, result) tuple -- TTL is now implicit in
  the bucket component of the key, so only the result dict is stored.

commit.message:
Fix closure cell leaks, for-let force_local_let restore, and ClearLocalCells opcode

Three interrelated fixes for block-scoped closure/TDZ correctness:

1. Save/restore block_stmt_depth and force_local_let in all 6 function
   compilation paths (FnDecl, arrow, fn expr, async gen, gen, class ctor)
   so these states don't leak from enclosing blocks into nested functions.

2. Add ClearLocalCells opcode (110) to clean up stale closure cells between
   loop iterations and at block scope exits. Emitted in for-of, for-in,
   destructuring loops, and block statements. Prevents cross-iteration cell
   leaking at the top level.

3. Fix for-statement init clause to save/restore force_local_let instead of
   unconditionally resetting it to false. A for(let i...) inside a bare
   block (which sets force_local_let=true) was destroying the outer block's
   state, causing subsequent class declarations to emit DefineGlobal instead
   of SetLocal — leaving their pre-declared TDZ cells uninitialized.

4. Remove is_module_mode guard on MakeClosure top-level cell persistence
   so script-mode closures also get live bindings (not snapshots).

commit.message:
Make Linux wipe script safer for network filesystems (#41812)

This pull request enhances the safety of the `linux_wipe.sh` script by
ensuring that destructive file operations do not affect network-mounted
filesystems. The changes introduce checks to detect network filesystems,
prevent accidental deletion of remote data, and improve the reliability
of wipe operations by avoiding crossing filesystem boundaries.

**Network filesystem safety improvements:**

* Added a `NETWORK_FS_TYPES` variable and functions to detect and
unmount network filesystems, preventing the script from deleting data on
NFS, CIFS, SMB, SSHFS, and similar mounts.
(`ee/server/service/embedded_scripts/linux_wipe.sh`)
[[1]](diffhunk://#diff-7ac85220cbd45e63481837a405dacf198822a4fbf885b88f89b9bc870c947fccR3-R4)
[[2]](diffhunk://#diff-7ac85220cbd45e63481837a405dacf198822a4fbf885b88f89b9bc870c947fccR17-R84)
* Introduced an `unmount_network_filesystems` function called before
wiping operations to unmount all detected network filesystems.
(`ee/server/service/embedded_scripts/linux_wipe.sh`)
* Added an `is_network_mount` function to skip wiping any path residing
on a network filesystem.
(`ee/server/service/embedded_scripts/linux_wipe.sh`)

**Safe file deletion enhancements:**

* Implemented a `safe_rm` function that ensures file deletions do not
cross filesystem boundaries, using `rm --one-file-system` or `find
-xdev` as a fallback. All destructive operations now use this wrapper.
(`ee/server/service/embedded_scripts/linux_wipe.sh`)
* Updated `wipe_non_essential_data` and `wipe_system_files` to use
`safe_rm` and to skip paths on network filesystems.
(`ee/server/service/embedded_scripts/linux_wipe.sh`)

These changes significantly reduce the risk of deleting data on remote
or shared filesystems during a wipe operation.

commit.message:
EXP-1231-1240: pipeline diagnostics and clinical validation

Results:
- EXP-1231: Ensemble alignment bug (documented, EXP-1211 SOTA stands)
- EXP-1232: ACF(1)=0.48, ACF(6)≈0 — AR only works at short horizons
- EXP-1233: Dawn conditioning provides zero benefit (-0.001)
- EXP-1234: Conformal PIs perfectly calibrated (80%→79.7%, 90%→89.9%, 95%→95.3%)
- EXP-1235: U-shaped error profile (Q3 lowest, Q4 highest)
- EXP-1236: 3-model ensemble optimal (+0.028), 5-model adds nothing
- EXP-1237: AR coefficients ≈ 0 at 60-min horizon (confirms ACF analysis)
- EXP-1238: Online learning marginal (+0.004, helps high-NaN patients)
- EXP-1239: Systematic +5mg bias at low glucose (calibration opportunity)
- EXP-1240: MARD=20.1%, Clarke Zone A=64.1%, False Safe=0.63%

Key insight: Ensemble+AR works because short-horizon sub-models
have recent AR residuals where autocorrelation is still significant.
At 60-min lag, autocorrelation vanishes completely.

Replace Fisher's exact test with junction origin classification (#6)

* Switch test data to matched gastric cancer tumor/normal pair

Replace paired-end ERR188273 (GEUVADIS LCL) with single-end matched
gastric cancer samples from ENA:
  - SRR9143066: Primary Tumor (gastric cancer surgical section)
  - SRR9143065: Solid Tissue Normal (adjacent stomach tissue)

Both samples are freely available via ENA HTTPS. Single-end layout is
handled by the existing HISAT2 rule (empty fastq2 → -U mode).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* Refactor filter_junctions: provenance-based classification

Replace per-sample novelty filtering with cross-sample classification.
Unannotated tumor junctions are now labeled by provenance:
  - tumor_specific:   absent in matched normal → neoepitope candidates
  - patient_specific: also present in normal   → excluded downstream

Normal samples are consumed internally to build an exclusion set and do
not appear in the output TSV. When no normal sample is present, all
unannotated junctions are labeled tumor_specific with a warning.

Adds min_normal_reads param (default: 2) to filter.smk and config.yaml
to avoid false patient_specific labels from single-read noise in normal.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* Filter assemble_contigs to tumor_specific junctions only

patient_specific junctions (present in matched normal) are now excluded
from contig assembly and all downstream neoepitope prediction steps.
They remain in the novel_junctions.tsv for provenance reporting.

Gracefully skips the filter when junction_origin column is absent
(backwards-compatible with older junction TSVs).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* Simplify reporting: replace statistical analysis with provenance summary

Remove statistical_analysis and summarise_all rules (and their dependency
on Fisher's exact test). Replace generate_report with a lightweight HTML
report showing:
  - junction provenance counts (tumor_specific vs patient_specific)
  - binder class summary (strong / weak / non)
  - top strong binders table

The summary_table.tsv target is removed from rule all; the per-run
report.html is sufficient for result verification.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* Remove cancer_types_with_normal config; add min_normal_reads to test config

cancer_types_with_normal was only used by the now-removed statistical_analysis
rule. Normal sample handling is now implicit: any sample with 'Normal' in its
sample_type is used as the exclusion set in filter_junctions.

min_normal_reads (default: 2) is now explicit in test_config.yaml to match
the production config.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* Replace 'provenance' with 'origin' throughout codebase

'Origin' is the standard term in cancer genomics for describing where
a junction comes from. 'Provenance' is a data-management term that does
not belong in biological code.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit.message:
[Build] Prefer Ninja over Make in build.sh (#364)

* [Build] Prefer Ninja over Make in build.sh

Ninja provides significantly better build performance for MLIR-based
projects compared to Unix Makefiles:

- Faster incremental builds: Ninja's dependency graph resolution is
  more efficient, which matters for projects with heavy TableGen
  (.td -> .inc) codegen pipelines.
- Better parallelism: Ninja uses a global dependency graph with global
  parallel scheduling, avoiding the serialization bottleneck of Make's
  recursive subdirectory traversal.
- More precise rebuilds: Ninja avoids unnecessary rebuilds caused by
  timestamp-based staleness checks on generated .inc files.
- LLVM/MLIR recommended: Ninja is the officially recommended generator
  for LLVM/MLIR projects and is used in upstream CI.

Falls back to Unix Makefiles when ninja is not installed.

Made-with: Cursor

* [Build] Reuse cached CMake generator to avoid mismatch errors

When a CMakeCache.txt already exists in the build directory, read the
cached CMAKE_GENERATOR and reuse it instead of re-detecting. This
prevents CMake from erroring out when the environment changes (e.g.
ninja installed/uninstalled after initial configure).

Fresh builds still auto-detect Ninja when available.

Made-with: Cursor

* Update scripts/build.sh

commit.message:
ci: avoid duplicate runs on PR branches (#12979)

Previously both 'push' and 'pull_request' triggers fired for every commit
on a PR branch, causing two concurrent check runs. Restrict 'push' to
master and release branches only — these are the post-merge confirmation
runs. PRs are covered exclusively by the 'pull_request' trigger.
